title: Summit Ridge
lead: A skilled local contractor with fifteen years of quality work and a digital presence that made them look like they'd just started. We rebuilt everything from the ground up.

sections.heading: Fifteen years of work. Zero online presence worth mentioning.
paragraphs: Summit Ridge had built a strong local reputation the old-fashioned way — word of mouth, handshakes, and work that spoke for itself. Their crew was experienced, their jobs were clean, and their customers referred them constantly. But anyone who didn't already know them? They were invisible.

The owner had been putting off the brand and website for years. He knew it needed to happen — he just didn't have the time, and every agency he'd spoken to wanted a budget that didn't make sense for a small contracting operation. **The work was there. The brand wasn't.**

callout.label: The Problem
callout.body: A logo made in Microsoft Paint, a Facebook business page acting as the only web presence, no consistent visual identity, and zero photos of actual completed work. Homeowners researching contractors online simply couldn't take them seriously.

sections.heading: Built for trust, not for trends.
paragraphs: Contracting is a high-trust purchase. Homeowners aren't just hiring someone to do work — they're letting strangers into their house and trusting them with a significant investment. The brand needed to communicate reliability, experience, and professionalism without looking like every other contractor who slapped a hard hat on a clip art logo.

We kept it grounded. **Dark palette, strong typography, clean layout.** Nothing gimmicky. The kind of brand that makes someone think "these people know what they're doing" before they ever pick up the phone.

callout.label: The Direction
callout.body: Professional and straightforward. A brand that looks established, not startup-fresh. One that earns trust by not trying too hard.

sections.heading: A mark they could put on everything.
paragraphs: We started with the logo — something that could live on a truck door, a yard sign, a hard hat sticker, and a website header and look equally at home in all four. From there we built out the full system: colors, type, and usage guidelines so every touchpoint stayed consistent without anyone having to think about it.
beforeItems: No truck or yard sign branding
Quotes sent as plain text emails
afterItems: Clean logo with full + icon versions
Truck wrap template + yard sign file

sections.heading: A site that works like a salesperson.
paragraphs: The website had one job: when a homeowner in Pike County Googles "contractor near me" and clicks through, they should immediately feel like they're in good hands. Services clearly listed, completed work front and center, and a contact form that actually gets used.
We kept it simple by design. **One page, all the essentials** — no bloated CMS, no plugins to maintain, nothing that would break six months after launch. Fast, clean, and easy to find.

sections.heading: Show the work, not just the logo.
paragraphs: Contractors live and die by their portfolio. Every homeowner considering hiring Summit Ridge wanted to see finished jobs — not stock photos of construction workers, not blurry phone shots, but **clean, well-lit images of real completed projects** that demonstrated the quality of their actual work.

We shot a half-day session across two recently completed job sites — a deck build and a full basement renovation — plus some on-site crew shots for the website's about section. Everything captured with the brand's visual direction in mind.

pullQuote.text: "I've been meaning to get real photos of our work for ten years. Now I actually want to show people the website."

sections.heading: Looks like what they always were.
paragraphs: Summit Ridge launched with a complete brand system, a live website, an updated Google Business Profile, and a library of job-site photography — everything a homeowner needs to say yes before they ever make a call.

The owner reported that within the first month, multiple new clients mentioned finding them through Google — something that hadn't happened before. **The work didn't change. The way people found and evaluated them did.**

callout.label: What They Left With
callout.body: A brand they own outright, a website that requires no ongoing subscription or maintenance contract, a full photography library, and a Google presence that actually shows up. All in five weeks, start to finish.

cta.heading: Ready to look like
cta.sub: Every project starts with a conversation. Reach out and I'll get back to you within 24 hours.
